title: rblp Development Notes
text: rblp R package implementing BLP (Berry, Levinsohn, Pakes 1995) demand estimation differentiated products. translated pyblp (Conlon & Gortmaker 2020).

title: rblp Development Notes
text: R6 classes: BLPFormulation, BLPEconomy, BLPProblem, BLPResults, BLPSimulation BLPProblem extends BLPEconomy (inherits data setup, adds solve methods) Main files: R/problem.R (solver), R/economy.R (base class), R/results.R (post-estimation), R/formulation.R (model specification), R/simulation.R (data generation)

title: rblp Development Notes
text: Product data must : market_ids, shares, prices, firm_ids, demand_instruments0..N Agent data must : market_ids, weights, plus demographic columns blp_formulation(~ prices + x, absorb = ~ product_ids) fixed effects Integration: blp_integration("product", size = N) Gauss-Hermite Optimization: blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b", method_options = list(maxit = 200))

title: rblp Development Notes
text: pyblp Formulation('0 + prices', absorb='C(product_ids)') → blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ product_ids) pyblp Problem([f1, f2, f3], product_data, agent_formulation, agent_data) → blp_problem(list(f1, f2), products, agent_formulation = f3, agent_data = agents)

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: vignette demonstrates advanced rblp features go beyond basic estimation: aggregate elasticities, cost passthrough, long-run diversion, optimal instruments, parametric bootstrap, importance sampling. tools essential policy analysis robustness checks.

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
code: library(rblp)  products <- load_nevo_products() f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) problem <- blp_problem(list(f1), products) results <- problem$solve(method = "2s") results$summary_table()[, c("parameter", "estimate", "se")] #>     parameter     estimate          se #> 1 (Intercept)  -2.78233619 0.107316633 #> 2      prices -11.75633541 0.845317475 #> 3       sugar   0.04752765 0.004146691 #> 4       mushy   0.05934698 0.051360224

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: aggregate elasticity measures percentage change total inside market share uniform 1% increase prices. Unlike J×JJ \times J product-level elasticity matrix, scalar per market — useful quick summaries. aggregate elasticities negative: inside goods become expensive, consumers substitute toward outside option. specific market:
code:
ae <- results$compute_aggregate_elasticities()  cat(sprintf("Aggregate elasticities across %d markets:
", length(ae))) #> Aggregate elasticities across 94 markets: cat(sprintf("  Mean: %.4f
", mean(ae))) #>   Mean: -0.7501 cat(sprintf("  Range: [%.4f, %.4f]
", min(ae), max(ae))) #>   Range: [-1.2829, -0.4076] ae_m1 <- results$compute_aggregate_elasticities(   market_id = problem$unique_market_ids[1] ) cat(sprintf("Market '%s' aggregate elasticity: %.4f
",             problem$unique_market_ids[1], ae_m1)) #> Market 'C01Q1' aggregate elasticity: -0.8302

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: passthrough matrix dp/dcdp/dc measures $1 increase marginal cost product kk changes equilibrium price product jj. Bertrand-Nash pricing, derived implicit function theorem applied system first-order conditions. perfect competition, -cost passthrough equals 1 (costs fully passed prices). oligopoly, typically less 1 — firms absorb cost increase.
code:
t1 <- problem$unique_market_ids[1] PT <- results$compute_passthrough(t1)  cat(sprintf("Passthrough matrix: %d x %d
", nrow(PT), ncol(PT))) #> Passthrough matrix: 24 x 24 cat("
Own-cost passthrough (diagonal, first 5 products):
") #>  #> Own-cost passthrough (diagonal, first 5 products): cat(sprintf("  %s
", paste(round(diag(PT)[1:5], 4), collapse = ", "))) #>   1.1685, 1.1002, 1.1776, 1.0723, 1.2613

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: Standard (short-run) diversion ratios hold rivals’ prices fixed. Long-run diversion accounts equilibrium price adjustments: product jj’s cost rises price increases, rivals also adjust prices. relevant merger analysis. Long-run diversion typically larger rivals raise prices focal product becomes expensive, redirecting even demand.
code: D_sr <- results$compute_diversion_ratios(t1) D_lr <- results$compute_long_run_diversion_ratios(t1)  cat("Short-run vs Long-run diversion (product 1 -> products 2-5):\n") #> Short-run vs Long-run diversion (product 1 -> products 2-5): for (k in 2:5) {   cat(sprintf("  1 -> %d: SR = %.4f, LR = %.4f\n",               k, D_sr[k, 1], D_lr[k, 1])) } #>   1 -> 2: SR = 0.0125, LR = 0.0142 #>   1 -> 3: SR = 0.0126, LR = 0.0143 #>   1 -> 4: SR = 0.0125, LR = 0.0142 #>   1 -> 5: SR = 0.0126, LR = 0.0144

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
code:
s_pred <- results$compute_shares() profits <- results$compute_profits()  cat(sprintf("Predicted shares: range [%.6f, %.6f]
", min(s_pred), max(s_pred))) #> Predicted shares: range [0.000182, 0.446883] cat(sprintf("Correlation with observed: %.6f
",             cor(s_pred, problem$products$shares))) #> Correlation with observed: 1.000000 cat(sprintf("Profits: range [%.6f, %.6f]
", min(profits), max(profits))) #> Profits: range [0.000019, 0.084624]

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: Standard BLP instruments (sums rival characteristics) may weak. Optimal instruments (Chamberlain 1987, BLP 1999) project Jacobian structural error onto instrument space, yielding efficient GMM estimates.
code: oi <- results$compute_optimal_instruments() cat(sprintf("Optimal demand instruments: %d x %d\n",             nrow(oi$optimal_demand_instruments),             ncol(oi$optimal_demand_instruments))) #> Optimal demand instruments: 2256 x 4  # Re-estimate with optimal instruments prob_opt <- oi$to_problem() res_opt <- prob_opt$solve(method = "2s")  # Compare SEs se_standard <- results$summary_table()$se se_optimal <- res_opt$summary_table()$se comp <- data.frame(   parameter = results$summary_table()$parameter,   SE_standard = round(se_standard, 4),   SE_optimal = round(se_optimal, 4),   ratio = round(se_optimal / se_standard, 4) ) print(comp, row.names = FALSE) #>    parameter SE_standard SE_optimal  ratio #>  (Intercept)      0.1073     0.1094 1.0197 #>       prices      0.8453     0.8584 1.0155 #>        sugar      0.0041     0.0042 1.0147 #>        mushy      0.0514     0.0527 1.0264

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: parametric bootstrap provides standard errors post-estimation quantities (elasticities, CS, merger effects) resampling structural errors re-estimating:
code:
# Use a simple simulation for speed id_data <- build_id_data(T = 10, J = 8, F = 2) set.seed(42) id_data$x <- runif(nrow(id_data))  sim <- blp_simulation(list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x)), id_data,                       beta = c(0.5, -2.0, 0.8), xi_variance = 0.2, seed = 42) sim_res <- sim$replace_endogenous() sim_prob <- sim_res$to_problem() est <- sim_prob$solve(method = "1s")  # 10 bootstrap draws (use more in practice) boot <- est$bootstrap(draws = 10, seed = 123, method = "1s") cat(sprintf("Successful bootstrap draws: %d
", length(boot))) #> Successful bootstrap draws: 10  # Bootstrap distribution of price coefficient boot_prices <- sapply(boot, function(b) b$beta[2]) cat(sprintf("Price coefficient:
")) #> Price coefficient: cat(sprintf("  Point estimate: %.4f
", est$beta[2])) #>   Point estimate: 1.5240 cat(sprintf("  Bootstrap mean: %.4f
", mean(boot_prices))) #>   Bootstrap mean: 2.4238 cat(sprintf("  Bootstrap SD:   %.4f
", sd(boot_prices))) #>   Bootstrap SD:   2.8593 cat(sprintf("  Bootstrap 95%% CI: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             quantile(boot_prices, 0.025), quantile(boot_prices, 0.975))) #>   Bootstrap 95% CI: [-1.1730, 7.5441]

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: Importance sampling concentrates integration nodes regions high probability mass, improving accuracy fewer nodes. useful RC models mixing distribution moved far standard normal.
code:
# RC model for importance sampling sim_rc <- blp_simulation(   list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x), blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices + x)),   id_data, beta = c(0.5, -2.0, 0.8), sigma = diag(c(0.3, 0.3)),   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3),   xi_variance = 0.2, seed = 42 ) sim_rc_res <- sim_rc$replace_endogenous() prob_rc <- sim_rc_res$to_problem() est_rc <- prob_rc$solve(sigma = diag(c(0.3, 0.3)), method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b", method_options = list(maxit = 50)))  is_result <- est_rc$importance_sampling(n_draws = 100, seed = 42) cat(sprintf("Importance sampling:
")) #> Importance sampling: cat(sprintf("  Draws: %d
", nrow(is_result$nodes))) #>   Draws: 100 cat(sprintf("  Effective sample size: %.1f (of %d)
",             is_result$effective_sample_size, nrow(is_result$nodes))) #>   Effective sample size: 3.4 (of 100)  # Re-estimate with importance-sampled nodes prob_is <- is_result$to_problem() cat(sprintf("  Problem with IS: I = %d agents
", prob_is$I)) #>   Problem with IS: I = 1000 agents

title: Advanced Features: Optimal Instruments, Bootstrap, and More
text: initial_update option evaluates model starting parameter values updates weighting matrix first GMM step. especially useful initial W=(Z′Z/N)−1W = (Z'Z/N)^{-1} poor approximation:
code: f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) prob <- blp_problem(list(f1), load_nevo_products())  res_no_update <- prob$solve(method = "2s", initial_update = FALSE) res_with_update <- prob$solve(method = "2s", initial_update = TRUE)  cat(sprintf("Without initial_update: obj = %.4f\n", res_no_update$objective)) #> Without initial_update: obj = 139.8994 cat(sprintf("With initial_update:    obj = %.4f\n", res_with_update$objective)) #> With initial_update:    obj = 139.9303

title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: vignette replicates analysis Lei Ma’s baby_BLP repository using rblp. baby_BLP project pedagogical implementation BLP demand estimation applied --counter (OTC) drug data. walk full progression plain logit BLP random coefficients, showing step addresses different identification concern. data contains weekly sales 11 OTC pain relief products (Tylenol, Advil, Bayer, Store brand) across 2 stores 48 weeks, yielding 96 store-week markets 1,056 product-market observations.

title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: inside shares tiny (0.005–0.3%), meaning outside option (buying OTC drugs week) captures 99% market. typical narrowly-defined product categories.
code:
library(rblp)  products <- load_otc_products()  cat(sprintf("Observations: %d
", nrow(products))) #> Observations: 1056 cat(sprintf("Markets: %d
", length(unique(products$market_ids)))) #> Markets: 96 cat(sprintf("Products per market: %d
",             nrow(products) / length(unique(products$market_ids)))) #> Products per market: 11 cat(sprintf("Share range: [%.6f, %.6f]
", min(products$shares), max(products$shares))) #> Share range: [0.000052, 0.002854] cat(sprintf("Outside share (market 1): %.4f
", 1 - sum(products$shares[products$market_ids == "1"]))) #> Outside share (market 1): 0.9941

title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: plain logit regression : log(sjt)−log(s0t)=α⋅pjt+β⋅promojt+ξjt\log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) = \alpha \cdot p_{jt} + \beta \cdot \text{promo}_{jt} + \xi_{jt} Without product fixed effects, brand-level quality (ξj\xi_j) error term. Premium brands (Tylenol, Advil) higher quality higher prices, price positively correlated ξ\xi. creates omitted variable bias pushes price coefficient toward zero even makes positive. price coefficient near zero positive — exactly bias expected. logit model separate effect price unobserved product quality without additional controls.
code: f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + promotion) prob_nfe <- blp_problem(list(f1), products) res_nfe <- prob_nfe$solve(method = "1s") res_nfe$summary_table()[, c("parameter", "estimate", "se")] #>     parameter   estimate         se #> 1 (Intercept) -0.4757702 0.03600143 #> 2      prices -1.6469138 0.02711686 #> 3   promotion -0.8494480 0.30549565

what: Step 2: Logit with Product Fixed Effects
title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: Adding product fixed effects absorbs time-invariant product quality, removing main source omitted variable bias: log(sjt)−log(s0t)=α⋅pjt+β⋅promojt+FEj+ξjt\log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) = \alpha \cdot p_{jt} + \beta \cdot \text{promo}_{jt} + \text{FE}_j + \xi_{jt} Now price coefficient negative — consumers buy less prices higher, economic theory predicts. product fixed effects control fact Tylenol popular expensive.
code: f1_fe <- blp_formulation(~ prices + promotion, absorb = ~ product) prob_fe <- blp_problem(list(f1_fe), products) res_fe <- prob_fe$solve(method = "1s") res_fe$summary_table()[, c("parameter", "estimate", "se")] #>   parameter   estimate         se #> 1    prices -0.0762170 0.02321983 #> 2 promotion  0.2248348 0.06849685

what: Step 3: IV Logit with Cost Instruments
title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: Even product FE, remaining price variation may endogenous firms set prices response time-varying demand shocks (ξjt\xi_{jt}). baby_BLP approach instruments price using wholesale cost: Note: dataset, cost may strong valid instrument. baby_BLP README states “price assumed exogenous,” suggesting data comes retail scanner context prices set headquarters, response local demand shocks. IV results interpreted caveat.
code: products_iv <- products products_iv$demand_instruments0 <- products_iv$cost  f1_iv <- blp_formulation(~ prices + promotion, absorb = ~ product) prob_iv <- blp_problem(list(f1_iv), products_iv) res_iv <- prob_iv$solve(method = "2s") res_iv$summary_table()[, c("parameter", "estimate", "se")] #>   parameter  estimate         se #> 1    prices 0.1685500 0.11681658 #> 2 promotion 0.3078086 0.08798796

title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: BLP model adds consumer heterogeneity random coefficient intercept (constant term). allows consumers stronger baseline preference purchasing OTC drugs, breaking logit’s restrictive IIA (Independence Irrelevant Alternatives) substitution pattern. Following baby_blp.jl specification: X1 (linear parameters): price + promotion + product FE X2 (random coefficient): intercept (σ\sigma scalar) Instruments: exogenous regressors + cost Integration: Gauss-Hermite product rule Interpretation sigma: positive sigma constant means consumers differ baseline propensity buy OTC drugs. consumers frequent buyers (high intercept), others rarely purchase (low intercept). heterogeneity generates realistic substitution patterns plain logit.
code:
f1_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + promotion, absorb = ~ product) f2_rc <- blp_formulation(~ 1)  # random coefficient on constant only  prob_rc <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1_rc, f2_rc),   product_data = products_iv,   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 7) )  cat(sprintf("K1=%d (linear), K2=%d (random), MD=%d (instruments), I=%d (agents)
",             prob_rc$K1, prob_rc$K2, prob_rc$MD, prob_rc$I)) #> K1=2 (linear), K2=1 (random), MD=2 (instruments), I=672 (agents)  res_rc <- prob_rc$solve(   sigma = matrix(1, 1, 1),  # starting value from baby_blp.jl   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 500)) )  res_rc$summary_table()[, c("parameter", "estimate", "se")] #>    parameter  estimate           se #> 1     prices 0.1674598 1.168622e-01 #> 2  promotion 0.3078427 8.807058e-02 #> 3 sigma[1,1] 1.0000000 4.225193e+16 cat(sprintf("Sigma (RC std dev on constant): %.4f
", res_rc$sigma[1, 1])) #> Sigma (RC std dev on constant): 1.0000 cat(sprintf("GMM objective: %.6f
", res_rc$objective)) #> GMM objective: 0.000000 cat(sprintf("Converged: %s
", res_rc$optimization_converged)) #> Converged: TRUE

title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: logit product FE gives well-identified elasticities: plain logit, cross-price elasticities within column identical (IIA property). product 1’s price increases, every product gains proportional share — regardless whether close substitute. BLP random coefficients model relaxes allowing substitution depend product similarity characteristic space.

E <- res_fe$compute_elasticities("1")  # market 1  cat("Own-price elasticities (market 1):

", min(diag(E)), max(diag(E)))) #>   Range: [-0.621, -0.142] cat(sprintf("  Mean:  %.3f
", mean(diag(E)))) #>   Mean:  -0.340  # IIA check: in logit, cross-elasticities in each column are identical cross_12 <- E[-(1:2), 1] cat(sprintf("
Cross-price elasticities w.r.t. product 1: %.6f (all identical = IIA)
",             cross_12[1])) #>  #> Cross-price elasticities w.r.t. product 1: 0.000283 (all identical = IIA)

title: Replicating baby_BLP: OTC Drug Demand Estimation
text: Key takeaways: Without product FE, price coefficient biased toward zero (positive) due omitted brand quality. Product FE corrects , yielding negative price coefficient. IV specification (using cost) moves coefficient , though instrument may weak context. RC model adds consumer heterogeneity, enriching substitution patterns beyond logit’s IIA restriction.
code: results <- data.frame(   Specification = c("Logit (no FE)", "Logit + Product FE",                      "IV + Product FE", "RC + Product FE"),   Price = c(res_nfe$beta[which(colnames(prob_nfe$products$X1) == "prices")],             res_fe$beta[1], res_iv$beta[1], res_rc$beta[1]),   Promotion = c(res_nfe$beta[which(colnames(prob_nfe$products$X1) == "promotion")],                 res_fe$beta[2], res_iv$beta[2], res_rc$beta[2]),   Objective = c(res_nfe$objective, res_fe$objective,                 res_iv$objective, res_rc$objective) ) results$Price <- round(results$Price, 4) results$Promotion <- round(results$Promotion, 4) results$Objective <- round(results$Objective, 4) print(results, row.names = FALSE) #>       Specification   Price Promotion Objective #>       Logit (no FE) -1.6469   -0.8494         0 #>  Logit + Product FE -0.0762    0.2248         0 #>     IV + Product FE  0.1686    0.3078         0 #>     RC + Product FE  0.1675    0.3078         0

title: Preparing Data for rblp
text: rblp expects product agent data specific format. guide explains prepare data BLP estimation. data.frame data.table inputs supported — rblp converts internally.

title: Preparing Data for rblp
text: product data must data frame (data.table) one row per product-market observation. following columns recognized:

title: Preparing Data for rblp
text: Excluded demand supply instruments detected name pattern: Exogenous product characteristics X1X_1 (everything except prices shares) automatically added instrument set. need supply excluded instruments — shift prices enter utility directly.

title: Preparing Data for rblp
text: additional numeric columns can used product characteristics formulations (e.g., sugar, mushy, hpwt, mpd, space).

what: Example: Building Product Data from Scratch
title: Preparing Data for rblp
code:
library(rblp)  # Suppose you have raw sales data set.seed(42) raw <- data.frame(   city     = rep(c("NYC", "LA", "CHI"), each = 30),   quarter  = rep(rep(1:2, each = 15), 3),   brand    = rep(paste0("brand_", 1:5), 18),   firm     = rep(c("A", "A", "B", "B", "C"), 18),   units    = rpois(90, lambda = 500),   pop      = rep(c(1e6, 8e5, 5e5), each = 30),   price    = runif(90, 2, 8),   sugar    = rep(runif(5, 1, 15), 18),   cost     = runif(90, 1, 5) )  # Step 1: Create market identifier raw$market_ids <- paste(raw$city, raw$quarter, sep = "_")  # Step 2: Compute market shares # Market share = units / market size (population) # The outside share s_0 = 1 - sum(s_j) must be positive! raw$shares <- raw$units / raw$pop  # Verify: inside shares sum to < 1 in each market inside_totals <- tapply(raw$shares, raw$market_ids, sum) stopifnot(all(inside_totals < 1)) cat("Inside share range:", range(inside_totals), "
") #> Inside share range: 0.007537 0.015024  # Step 3: Rename columns to match rblp conventions products <- data.frame(   market_ids = raw$market_ids,   firm_ids   = raw$firm,   product_ids = raw$brand,   shares     = raw$shares,   prices     = raw$price,   sugar      = raw$sugar,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Step 4: Add instruments # BLP instruments: sums of rival/own-firm characteristics X_exog <- as.matrix(products[, "sugar", drop = FALSE]) blp_iv <- build_blp_instruments(X_exog, products$market_ids, products$firm_ids) for (k in seq_len(ncol(blp_iv))) {   products[[paste0("demand_instruments", k - 1)]] <- blp_iv[, k] }  cat("Product data ready:", nrow(products), "observations
") #> Product data ready: 90 observations cat("Columns:", paste(names(products), collapse = ", "), "
") #> Columns: market_ids, firm_ids, product_ids, shares, prices, sugar, demand_instruments0, demand_instruments1

title: Preparing Data for rblp
text: rblp accepts data.table objects directly. conversion needed:
code: library(data.table)  # Read data as data.table products <- fread("my_data.csv")  # Add columns using data.table syntax products[, market_ids := paste(city, quarter, sep = "_")] products[, shares := units / pop]  # Build instruments X_exog <- as.matrix(products[, .(sugar)]) iv <- build_blp_instruments(X_exog, products$market_ids, products$firm_ids) products[, paste0("demand_instruments", 0:(ncol(iv)-1)) := as.data.frame(iv)]  # Pass directly to blp_problem (no as.data.frame() needed) problem <- blp_problem(list(blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar)), products)

title: Preparing Data for rblp
text: Shares must strictly 0 1. Zero negative shares cause log(s) fail. Filter zero-sales observations add small positive constant. Inside shares must sum less 1. outside good share s0=1−∑jsjs_0 = 1 - \sum_j s_j must positive. shares sum 1, need redefine market size include non-purchasers. Price column must named prices (“s”). rblp identifies endogenous regressor instrument construction elasticity computation. Instrument columns must follow naming convention: demand_instruments0, demand_instruments1, etc. Arbitrary column names detected instruments. Agent weights must sum 1 within market. using blp_integration(), handled automatically. absorb = ~ product_ids, product_ids column must exist data. formula variable name must match column name.

title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: vignette covers simplest demand models BLP framework: plain logit logit product fixed effects. models serve useful starting points moving random coefficients specifications (see vignette("random-coefficients")).

title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: logit demand model, consumer ii market tt receives utility product jj: uijt=δjt+εijtu_{ijt} = \delta_{jt} + \varepsilon_{ijt} δjt=xjt′β+αpjt+ξjt\delta_{jt} = x_{jt}'\beta + \alpha p_{jt} + \xi_{jt} mean utility common across consumers, εijt\varepsilon_{ijt} ..d. Type Extreme Value (Gumbel) taste shock. term ξjt\xi_{jt} unobserved (econometrician) product-market quality. consumer chooses outside option (good 0) inside good provides higher utility ui0t=εi0tu_{i0t} = \varepsilon_{i0t}.

title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: Type EV assumption, market share product jj takes well-known logit form: sjt=exp(δjt)1+∑k=1Jtexp(δkt)s_{jt} = \frac{\exp(\delta_{jt})}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{J_t} \exp(\delta_{kt})} Berry (1994) showed can analytically inverted recover mean utilities observed shares: δjt=log(sjt)−log(s0t)\delta_{jt} = \log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) s0t=1−∑jsjts_{0t} = 1 - \sum_j s_{jt} outside good share. inversion exact requires iteration – major computational advantage plain logit.

title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: Substituting linear specification δjt\delta_{jt}, estimating equation : log(sjt)−log(s0t)=xjt′β+αpjt+ξjt\log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) = x_{jt}'\beta + \alpha p_{jt} + \xi_{jt} Prices pjtp_{jt} set firms observe ξjt\xi_{jt} (unobserved quality), creating endogeneity: Cov(pjt,ξjt)≠0\text{Cov}(p_{jt}, \xi_{jt}) \neq 0. OLS estimation α\alpha therefore biased (typically toward zero, understating price sensitivity). Instrumental variables (IV) needed consistently estimate price coefficient. moment condition : E[Zjt′ξjt]=0E[Z_{jt}' \xi_{jt}] = 0 ZjtZ_{jt} instruments correlated prices uncorrelated unobserved quality. Common choices include: BLP instruments: sums characteristics -firm rival products Differentiation instruments (Gandhi & Houde 2020): measures product isolation characteristic space Cost shifters: input prices, exchange rates, etc. Nevo cereal dataset comes 20 pre-computed excluded instruments (demand_instruments0 demand_instruments19).

what: Example: Plain Logit with Nevo Data
title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: plain logit includes intercept observable product characteristics linear index: Key observations: price coefficient (α\alpha) negative, expected: higher prices reduce utility. exogenous product characteristics (intercept, sugar, mushy) 20 pre-computed instruments serve excluded instruments price variable. IV-GMM estimator, OLS, addressing price endogeneity.
code:
library(rblp)  # Load the Nevo (2000) cereal data products <- load_nevo_products()  cat(sprintf("Observations: %d
", nrow(products))) #> Observations: 2256 cat(sprintf("Markets: %d
", length(unique(products$market_ids)))) #> Markets: 94 cat(sprintf("Products per market: %d
", length(unique(products$product_ids)))) #> Products per market: 24 # Define the linear demand formulation: intercept + prices + sugar + mushy f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  # Create the problem (single formulation = logit) logit_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1), products)  # Solve with one-step GMM logit_results <- logit_problem$solve(method = "1s")  print(logit_results) #> BLP Estimation Results #>   Method: 1S GMM #>   Objective: 2.047932e+02 #>   Optimization converged: TRUE #>   FP converged: TRUE (94 total iterations) #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>  parameter   estimate   se       t_stat  #>  (Intercept) -2.810674  0.109432 -25.684 #>  prices      -11.699731 0.858444 -13.629 #>  sugar       0.048381   0.004208 11.498  #>  mushy       0.043127   0.052717 0.818

what: Example: Logit with Product Fixed Effects
title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: panel data available (products observed across multiple markets/time periods), product fixed effects can absorb time-invariant product characteristics. within estimator applied logit share inversion. specification absorbs αj\alpha_j (product dummies), remaining coefficient price coefficient α\alpha: log(sjt)−log(s0t)=αj+αpjt+ξjt\log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) = \alpha_j + \alpha p_{jt} + \xi_{jt} rblp, absorb argument implements Frisch-Waugh-Lovell (FWL) theorem, demeaning variables within groups defined absorb formula. price coefficient -30.42 substantially negative plain logit estimate. product fixed effects control time-invariant unobserved quality, reducing upward bias positive correlation prices quality. result matches pyblp Nevo tutorial specification, analogous code :
code: # Absorb product-level fixed effects f1_fe <- blp_formulation(~ prices, absorb = ~ product_ids)  fe_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1_fe), products) fe_results <- fe_problem$solve(method = "1s")  print(fe_results) #> BLP Estimation Results #>   Method: 1S GMM #>   Objective: 1.797148e+02 #>   Optimization converged: TRUE #>   FP converged: TRUE (664 total iterations) #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>  parameter estimate   se       t_stat  #>  prices    -30.420493 1.030311 -29.526 # pyblp equivalent formulation = pyblp.Formulation('0 + prices', absorb='C(product_ids)')

title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: nested logit relaxes IIA grouping products nests (categories). Substitution within nest stronger substitution across nests. utility specification : uijt=δjt+ζigt+(1−ρ)εijtu_{ijt} = \delta_{jt} + \zeta_{igt} + (1 - \rho)\varepsilon_{ijt} gg indexes nest product jj belongs , ζigt\zeta_{igt} nest-specific shock, ρ∈[0,1)\rho \[0, 1) nesting parameter. ρ=0\rho = 0, nested logit reduces plain logit; ρ→1\rho \1, products within nest become perfect substitutes. share equation becomes: log(sjt)−log(s0t)=xjt′β+αpjt+ρlog(sj|g,t)+ξjt\log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) = x_{jt}'\beta + \alpha p_{jt} + \rho \log(s_{j|g,t}) + \xi_{jt} sj|g,ts_{j|g,t} within-nest share product jj.

title: Logit and Nested Logit Models
text: estimate nested logit rblp, include nesting_ids column product data pass rho parameter solve(): nested logit offers middle ground plain logit (restrictive substitution) full random coefficients model (computationally demanding). particularly useful products natural categorical structure (e.g., cereal brands manufacturer, cars segment).
code: # Suppose products have a 'nesting_ids' column (e.g., product category) # products$nesting_ids <- products$brand_category  # hypothetical  # Same formulation as logit f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) nested_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1), products)  # Solve with nesting parameter (starting value) nested_results <- nested_problem$solve(   rho = 0.5,  # starting value for the nesting parameter   method = "1s" )  # The estimated rho captures within-nest correlation print(nested_results)

title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: vignette walks exercises Mixtape Sessions Demand Estimation course, taught Jeff Gortmaker others. original course materials use Python pyblp; reproduce every exercise R using rblp package. exercises build progressively: Exercise 1 (Pure Logit): OLS logit, IV logit, fixed effects, counterfactual price cuts, elasticities. Exercise 2 (Mixed Logit): Adding demographic interactions random coefficients capture consumer heterogeneity. Exercise 3 (Micro Moments): Using individual-level data sharpen identification (conceptual overview). dataset simplified version Nevo (2000) ready--eat cereal data covering 24 products across 94 city-quarter markets.

what: Q1-Q2: Data Description and Market Share Computation
title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: Mixtape dataset panel 24 cereal products observed 94 city-quarter markets. product-market, observe quantity sold (servings_sold), city population, price per serving, one excluded instrument price. Market shares computed : sjt=servings_soldjtcity_populationt×90s_{jt} = \frac{\text{servings\_sold}_{jt}}{\text{city\_population}_t \times 90} 90 assumed number potential servings per person per quarter (one per day). defines total market size, outside good absorbing whatever fraction population purchase 24 products.

title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: plain logit model Berry (1994) linear form: log(sjt)−log(s0t)=β0+αpjt+β1mushyjt+ξjt\log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) = \beta_0 + \alpha \, p_{jt} + \beta_1 \, \text{mushy}_{jt} + \xi_{jt} first estimate OLS, biased prices pjtp_{jt} correlated unobserved product-market quality ξjt\xi_{jt} (price endogeneity). OLS estimates : Intercept: approximately −2.935-2.935 Price coefficient (α\alpha): approximately −7.48-7.48 Mushy: approximately 0.0750.075 (statistically significant) price coefficient biased toward zero. High-quality products command higher prices (positive correlation pjtp_{jt} ξjt\xi_{jt}), partially offsets true negative effect price demand.
code: # Compute the dependent variable: log(s_j) - log(s_0) outside_shares <- 1 - tapply(products$shares, products$market_ids, sum) products$s0 <- as.numeric(outside_shares[as.character(products$market_ids)]) products$y <- log(products$shares) - log(products$s0)  # OLS regression ols_fit <- lm(y ~ prices + mushy, data = products)  cat("=== OLS Logit Results ===\n\n") #> === OLS Logit Results === print(round(summary(ols_fit)$coefficients, 3)) #>             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) #> (Intercept)   -2.935      0.114 -25.694     0.00 #> prices        -7.480      0.863  -8.668     0.00 #> mushy          0.075      0.053   1.407     0.16

title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: can replicate OLS logit using blp_problem(). key set demand_instruments0 = prices, makes instrument set identical regressors (.e., excluded instruments), IV estimator reduces OLS. rblp results match lm() estimates exactly: confirms GMM framework identity instrument matrix reduces OLS.
code: # For OLS: use prices as own instrument (no excluded instruments) products_ols <- products products_ols$demand_instruments0 <- products_ols$prices  f1_ols <- blp_formulation(~ prices + mushy) ols_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1_ols), products_ols) ols_results <- ols_problem$solve(method = "1s")  cat("=== rblp OLS Logit Results ===\n\n") #> === rblp OLS Logit Results === print(ols_results) #> BLP Estimation Results #>   Method: 1S GMM #>   Objective: 1.336262e-24 #>   Optimization converged: TRUE #>   FP converged: TRUE (94 total iterations) #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>  parameter   estimate  se       t_stat  #>  (Intercept) -2.934501 0.107883 -27.201 #>  prices      -7.480136 0.839535 -8.910  #>  mushy       0.074765  0.054087 1.382

what: Q5: Fixed Effects Logit (Market + Product FE)
title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: Adding market product fixed effects absorbs time-invariant product characteristics market-level demand shifters. eliminates ξjt\xi_{jt} components purely product-specific purely market-specific, leaving within-product, within-market variation identification. fixed effects prices instrument, “FE logit” yet address endogeneity remaining price variation. price coefficient market product fixed effects approximately −28.6-28.6, much negative OLS estimate −7.48-7.48. fixed effects absorb product-level quality positively correlated prices, revealing much stronger true price sensitivity.
code: # FE logit: absorb market + product, prices as own instrument products_fe <- products products_fe$demand_instruments0 <- products_fe$prices  f1_fe <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ market_ids + product_ids) fe_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1_fe), products_fe) fe_results <- fe_problem$solve(method = "1s")  cat("=== FE Logit Results (prices as own IV) ===\n\n") #> === FE Logit Results (prices as own IV) === print(fe_results) #> BLP Estimation Results #>   Method: 1S GMM #>   Objective: 1.357330e-28 #>   Optimization converged: TRUE #>   FP converged: TRUE (664 total iterations) #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>  parameter estimate   se       t_stat  #>  prices    -28.617866 1.039270 -27.537

title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: now use IV + FE logit results simulate counterfactual: happens market shares product F1B04 receives price cut $0.04 per serving market C01Q1? (F1B04’s baseline price $0.072, roughly 55% price reduction.) pure logit, shares closed form given mean utilities δjt\delta_{jt}. price change product jj shifts mean utility Δδj=α×Δpj\Delta\delta_j = \alpha \times \Delta p_j, new shares follow logit formula. F1B04’s share increases dramatically (200%), products lose percent market share. large response reflects estimated price coefficient approximately −30.6-30.6: $0.04 price cut translates utility gain 0.04×30.6≈1.220.04 \times 30.6 \approx 1.22 units, substantial logit scale. pure logit, substitution pattern governed Independence Irrelevant Alternatives (IIA) property: competing products lose share proportion current shares, regardless similar F1B04. strength (simplicity) weakness (unrealistic substitution patterns) logit model. random coefficients model Exercise 2 relaxes IIA. Note: pyblp Mixtape solutions report F1B04 share change +223.6% products -1.45%. Small numerical differences arise optimizer tolerances GMM weighting matrix details.
code:
# Pick the first market first_market <- "C01Q1" mkt_idx <- which(products_iv$market_ids == first_market) mkt_data <- products_iv[mkt_idx, ]  # Current shares and delta s0_mkt <- unique(mkt_data$s0) current_delta <- log(mkt_data$shares) - log(s0_mkt) current_shares <- mkt_data$shares  # Price coefficient from IV + FE alpha <- iv_results$beta[1]  # Price cut of $0.04/serving for F1B04 new_prices <- mkt_data$prices f1b04_idx <- which(mkt_data$product_ids == "F1B04") price_cut <- 0.04 new_prices[f1b04_idx] <- new_prices[f1b04_idx] - price_cut  cat(sprintf("F1B04 price: $%.4f -> $%.4f (cut of $%.2f/serving)
",             mkt_data$prices[f1b04_idx], new_prices[f1b04_idx], price_cut)) #> F1B04 price: $0.0721 -> $0.0321 (cut of $0.04/serving)  # New delta: delta_new = delta_old + alpha * (p_new - p_old) new_delta <- current_delta + alpha * (new_prices - mkt_data$prices)  # New logit shares exp_delta <- exp(new_delta) new_shares <- exp_delta / (1 + sum(exp_delta))  # Percentage change in shares pct_change <- (new_shares - current_shares) / current_shares * 100  cat("
=== Counterfactual: $0.04 Price Cut for F1B04 in C01Q1 ===

") #>  #> === Counterfactual: $0.04 Price Cut for F1B04 in C01Q1 === cf_table <- data.frame(   product = mkt_data$product_ids,   old_share = sprintf("%.6f", current_shares),   new_share = sprintf("%.6f", new_shares),   pct_change = sprintf("%+.1f%%", pct_change),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) print(cf_table, row.names = FALSE, right = FALSE) #>  product old_share new_share pct_change #>  F1B04   0.012417  0.041005  +230.2%    #>  F1B06   0.007809  0.007583  -2.9%      #>  F1B07   0.012995  0.012618  -2.9%      #>  F1B09   0.005770  0.005603  -2.9%      #>  F1B11   0.017934  0.017415  -2.9%      #>  F1B13   0.026602  0.025832  -2.9%      #>  F1B17   0.025015  0.024291  -2.9%      #>  F1B30   0.005058  0.004912  -2.9%      #>  F1B45   0.005332  0.005177  -2.9%      #>  F2B05   0.038068  0.036966  -2.9%      #>  F2B08   0.008348  0.008106  -2.9%      #>  F2B15   0.006596  0.006405  -2.9%      #>  F2B16   0.030117  0.029246  -2.9%      #>  F2B19   0.100046  0.097150  -2.9%      #>  F2B26   0.013234  0.012851  -2.9%      #>  F2B28   0.023643  0.022958  -2.9%      #>  F2B40   0.008662  0.008411  -2.9%      #>  F2B48   0.002699  0.002621  -2.9%      #>  F3B06   0.018943  0.018395  -2.9%      #>  F3B14   0.010846  0.010532  -2.9%      #>  F4B02   0.007850  0.007623  -2.9%      #>  F4B10   0.000732  0.000711  -2.9%      #>  F4B12   0.009490  0.009216  -2.9%      #>  F6B18   0.046570  0.045222  -2.9%  cat(sprintf("

title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: -price elasticity logit model : ηjj=αpjt(1−sjt)\eta_{jj} = \alpha \, p_{jt} \, (1 - s_{jt}) Since α<0\alpha < 0, -price elasticities negative. compute using compute_elasticities() method. -price elasticities negative range approximately −2-2 −5-5 (roughly −2.4-2.4 −6.3-6.3 depending exact estimated alpha). expensive products elastic demand, makes economic sense: 1% price increase expensive cereal represents larger absolute price change, inducing consumers switch away.

title: Mixtape Demand Estimation Exercises
text: estimating full random coefficients, Mixtape exercises ask simple demographic interaction: valuation mushy cereals vary income? add log_income demographic interact mushy pi matrix. pyblp solution finds pi coefficient mushy x log_income approximately 0.251, indicating higher-income consumers (slightly) higher taste mushy cereals. Expected result: pi coefficient mushy x log_income approximately 0.251, meaning one-unit increase log income raises mean utility mushy cereals 0.25.
code: # Formulations f1_rc <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices + mushy, absorb = ~ market_ids + product_ids) f2_rc <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + mushy)  # Demographics formulation: log_income f_demo <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + log_income)  # Create problem rc_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1_rc, f2_rc),   product_data = products,   agent_formulation = f_demo,   agent_data = agents )  # Solve with pi on mushy x log_income # sigma = 0 (no unobserved heterogeneity), pi = initial guess pi0 <- matrix(0.1, nrow = 1, ncol = 1)  # K2=1 (mushy), D=1 (log_income)  rc_mushy_results <- rc_problem$solve(   sigma = matrix(0, 1, 1),  # no sigma (fixed at 0)   pi = pi0,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 500)) )  print(rc_mushy_results)

code: # X2 now includes price (for sigma) and mushy (for pi) f1_full <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices + mushy, absorb = ~ market_ids + product_ids) f2_full <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices + mushy)  # Demographics: log_income f_demo_full <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + log_income)  # Prepare products with proper IV products_rc <- products  # demand_instruments0 already = price_instrument  rc_full_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1_full, f2_full),   product_data = products_rc,   agent_formulation = f_demo_full,   agent_data = agents )  # Starting values near the pyblp solution # sigma: K2 x K2 = 2x2 diagonal (sigma on prices, 0 on mushy) sigma0 <- diag(c(5.0, 0))  # start sigma_price at 5  # pi: K2 x D = 2x1 (prices x log_income, mushy x log_income) pi0_full <- matrix(c(-5.0, 0.2), nrow = 2, ncol = 1)  rc_full_results <- rc_full_problem$solve(   sigma = sigma0,   pi = pi0_full,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 1000, factr = 1e7)) )  print(rc_full_results)

text: rblp provides three classes constructing micro moments: Micro moments can dramatically improve precision demographic interaction parameters (pi) random coefficient standard deviations (sigma) providing direct information buys . full treatment, see Conlon Gortmaker (2020, Section 4) pyblp micro moments tutorial.
code: # 1. Define a micro dataset (describes the data source) md <- micro_dataset(   name = "household scanner panel",   observations = 50000,   compute_weights = function(market_id, products, agents) {     # Return I x J weight matrix     I <- length(agents$weights)     J <- length(products$shares)     matrix(1, I, J)   } )  # 2. Define a micro part (what statistic to compute for each i,j pair) mp <- micro_part(   name = "income_x_mushy",   dataset = md,   compute_values = function(market_id, products, agents) {     # Return I x J value matrix: income_i * mushy_j     outer(agents$log_income, products$mushy)   } )  # 3. Define a micro moment (match target to prediction) mm <- micro_moment(   name = "E[log_income * mushy | purchase]",   value = 0.5,   # observed from micro data   parts = mp )  # Pass to solve() results <- rc_full_problem$solve(   sigma = sigma0,   pi = pi0_full,   micro_moments = list(mm),   method = "1s" )

title: Monte Carlo Validation of rblp
text: vignette validates rblp Monte Carlo simulation. generate data known data-generating processes (DGPs) verify estimator recovers true parameters across 1000+ replications. examine bias, RMSE, sign recovery, consistency, CI coverage. results produced tests/testthat/test-monte-carlo.R runs full simulation study. code shown eval=FALSE; run locally reproduce.

title: Monte Carlo Validation of rblp
code: library(rblp)  run_one_logit <- function(seed, T, J, F, true_beta, xi_var = 0.3) {   tryCatch({     id_data <- build_id_data(T = T, J = J, F = F)     set.seed(seed)     id_data$x <- runif(nrow(id_data))      sim <- blp_simulation(       list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x)), id_data,       beta = true_beta, xi_variance = xi_var, seed = seed     )     sim_res <- sim$replace_endogenous(       iteration = blp_iteration("simple", list(atol = 1e-12, max_evaluations = 3000)))     prob <- sim_res$to_problem()     est <- prob$solve(method = "2s")     list(beta = est$beta, se = est$summary_table()$se)   }, error = function(e) NULL) }  summarize_mc <- function(estimates, true_values, param_names) {   est_mat <- do.call(rbind, estimates)   n <- nrow(est_mat)   data.frame(     Parameter = param_names,     True = true_values,     Mean = round(colMeans(est_mat), 4),     Bias_Pct = round((colMeans(est_mat) - true_values) / abs(true_values) * 100, 1),     RMSE = round(sqrt((colMeans(est_mat) - true_values)^2 + apply(est_mat, 2, var)), 4),     Sign_Pct = round(colMeans(sign(est_mat) == sign(matrix(       true_values, n, length(true_values), byrow = TRUE))) * 100, 1)   ) }

title: Monte Carlo Validation of rblp
text: DGP: δjt=0.5−3.0⋅pjt+1.0⋅xj+ξjt\delta_{jt} = 0.5 - 3.0 \cdot p_{jt} + 1.0 \cdot x_j + \xi_{jt}, ξ∼N(0,0.3)\xi \sim N(0, 0.3), T=50 markets, J=20 products, F=4 firms. Prices endogenous (firms observe ξ\xi). BLP instruments (rival characteristic sums). Results (1000/1000 successful): Key findings: exogenous characteristics (x1,x2,x3x_1, x_2, x_3) recovered almost perfectly: bias <2%< 2\%, RMSE ≈0.06\approx 0.06, 100% sign recovery across 1000 replications. validates core GMM estimation machinery. price coefficient larger bias lower sign recovery. well-known weak instrument problem BLP — standard instruments (sums rival characteristics, differentiation IVs) limited first-stage power price equation. code bug; pyblp shows pattern. intercept poorly identified (common IV estimation constant hard instrument ).

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: random coefficients (RC) logit – also known mixed logit BLP model – workhorse modern demand estimation differentiated products. Unlike plain logit, generates flexible substitution patterns subject IIA property. vignette covers: model parameters (σ\sigma, π\pi) Integration methods simulating mixing distribution Estimation without demographics GMM methods convergence diagnostics plain logit nested logit, see vignette("logit-nested-logit"). post-estimation analysis, see vignette("post-estimation-mergers").

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: Consumer ii market tt receives utility product jj: uijt=δjt+μijt+εijtu_{ijt} = \delta_{jt} + \mu_{ijt} + \varepsilon_{ijt} : δjt=xjt′β+αpjt+ξjt\delta_{jt} = x_{jt}'\beta + \alpha p_{jt} + \xi_{jt} mean utility (common consumers) μijt=∑kxjkt(σkνik+πkdDid)\mu_{ijt} = \sum_k x_{jkt}(\sigma_k \nu_{ik} + \pi_{kd} D_{id}) individual deviation mean εijt\varepsilon_{ijt} Type EV idiosyncratic shock individual-specific component μijt\mu_{ijt} two sources heterogeneity: Unobserved heterogeneity (σ\sigma): νik∼N(0,1)\nu_{ik} \sim N(0, 1) standard normal draws, σk\sigma_k captures dispersion tastes characteristic kk Observed heterogeneity (π\pi): DidD_{id} observed demographics (income, age, etc.), πkd\pi_{kd} captures demographic dd shifts preferences characteristic kk

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: σ\sigma parameter K2×K2K_2 \times K_2 lower-triangular matrix (Cholesky root). many applications, σ\sigma restricted diagonal, meaning unobserved taste shocks different characteristics independent. Convention: specifying starting values sigma: Zero diagonal means parameter fixed zero (estimated) Non-zero values starting values optimization example, K2=4K_2 = 4 characteristics (intercept, prices, sugar, mushy):
code: # Diagonal sigma: all four RC variances are free sigma <- diag(c(0.5, 3.0, 0.01, 0.1))  # Fix sugar and mushy RC at zero, estimate only intercept and price RC sigma <- diag(c(0.5, 3.0, 0, 0))

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: demographics available, π\pi matrix K2×DK_2 \times D, DD number demographic variables. Element πkd\pi_{kd} captures demographic dd shifts taste coefficient characteristic kk.

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: RC logit requires numerical integration distribution unobserved heterogeneity ν\nu. choice integration method affects accuracy computation time.

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: product rule uses Gauss-Hermite quadrature nodes, taking tensor product across dimensions. size nodes per dimension K2K_2 random coefficients, yields sizeK2\text{size}^{K_2} integration points per market. product rule deterministic (simulation noise) provides exact integration polynomials degree 2×size−12 \times \text{size} - 1. However, suffers curse dimensionality: number points grows exponentially K2K_2.
code: # 5 nodes per dimension, K2=4 -> 5^4 = 625 points per market int_product <- blp_integration("product", size = 5)  # 3 nodes per dimension (faster, less accurate) int_small <- blp_integration("product", size = 3)  # 3^4 = 81 points

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: Standard Monte Carlo draws random points mixing distribution. size draws: Monte Carlo integration flexible introduces simulation noise can cause roughness GMM objective function, making optimization difficult.
code: # 1000 Monte Carlo draws per market int_mc <- blp_integration("monte_carlo", size = 1000, seed = 42)

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: Halton sequences quasi-random low-discrepancy sequences provide uniform coverage pseudo-random draws:
code: # 100 Halton draws per market (requires randtoolbox package) int_halton <- blp_integration("halton", size = 100, seed = 42)

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: applications: Small K2K_2 (2–4): product rule size = 5 size = 7 Moderate K2K_2 (5–8): Halton sequences 100–500 points Large K2K_2 (9+): Monte Carlo 1000+ draws

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: simplest RC logit unobserved heterogeneity (demographics). requires two formulations: one linear component (X1X_1) one nonlinear component (X2X_2).

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: problem 4 random coefficients (intercept, prices, sugar, mushy), size = 3 Gauss-Hermite nodes per dimension, get 34=813^4 = 81 integration points per market.
code:
library(rblp) products <- load_nevo_products()  # X1: linear demand (intercept + prices + sugar + mushy) f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  # X2: characteristics with random coefficients (same as X1 here) f2 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  # Create the problem with Gauss-Hermite integration rc_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1, f2),   product_data = products,   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3)  # 3^4 = 81 points )  cat(sprintf("K1 (linear params): %d
", rc_problem$K1)) #> K1 (linear params): 4 cat(sprintf("K2 (random coefficients): %d
", rc_problem$K2)) #> K2 (random coefficients): 4 cat(sprintf("Total integration nodes: %d (%d per market)
",             rc_problem$I, rc_problem$I / rc_problem$T)) #> Total integration nodes: 7614 (81 per market) cat(sprintf("Total observations: %d
", rc_problem$N)) #> Total observations: 2256 cat(sprintf("Markets: %d
", rc_problem$T)) #> Markets: 94

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: Interpretation: estimated σ̂k\hat{\sigma}_k values capture standard deviation taste heterogeneity characteristic Large σ̂price\hat{\sigma}_{\text{price}} means consumers differ substantially price sensitivity taste differences generate realistic substitution: consumers less price-sensitive substitute toward premium products
code: # Sigma starting values (4x4 diagonal) initial_sigma <- diag(c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5))  # Solve with one-step GMM rc_results <- rc_problem$solve(   sigma = initial_sigma,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 200, factr = 1e7)) )  print(rc_results)

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: sigma pi matrices encode parameter structure: Key points Nevo specification: agent data includes 20 agents per market pre-computed nodes (columns nodes0–nodes3), integration weights, demographic variables Product fixed effects X1X_1 absorb brand-level unobserved quality, price coefficient estimated linear part large number moment conditions (20 excluded instruments + exogenous characteristics) helps identify many nonlinear parameters Pi matrix zeros encode economic restrictions (e.g., income_squared interacts price coefficient)
code:
agents <- load_nevo_agents()  cat("Agent data columns:
") #> Agent data columns: cat(paste(names(agents), collapse = ", "), "

") #> market_ids, city_ids, quarter, weights, nodes0, nodes1, nodes2, nodes3, income, income_squared, age, child cat(sprintf("Agents: %d (%d per market)
", nrow(agents),             nrow(agents) / length(unique(agents$market_ids)))) #> Agents: 1880 (20 per market)  # Inspect the agent data head(agents[, c("market_ids", "weights", "income", "income_squared", "age", "child")]) #>   market_ids weights     income income_squared          age      child #> 1      C01Q1    0.05  0.4951235       8.331304 -0.230109009 -0.2308511 #> 2      C01Q1    0.05  0.3787622       6.121865 -2.532694102  0.7691489 #> 3      C01Q1    0.05  0.1050146       1.030803 -0.006965458 -0.2308511 #> 4      C01Q1    0.05 -1.4854809     -25.583605 -0.827946010  0.7691489 #> 5      C01Q1    0.05 -0.3165969      -6.517009 -0.230109009 -0.2308511 #> 6      C01Q1    0.05 -0.3372762      -6.878070  0.851696161 -0.2308511 # X1: linear demand with product fixed effects (absorb product dummies) # Only price is estimated; intercept, sugar, mushy are absorbed f1 <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ product_ids)  # X2: nonlinear demand (intercept + prices + sugar + mushy get RC) f2 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  # Agent formulation: demographics that interact with X2 f_demo <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + income + income_squared + age + child)  # Create the problem (agent data provides nodes, weights, and demographics) demo_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1, f2),   product_data = products,   agent_formulation = f_demo,   agent_data = agents )  cat(sprintf("K1 (linear params): %d (only prices, FE absorbed)
", demo_problem$K1)) #> K1 (linear params): 1 (only prices, FE absorbed) cat(sprintf("K2 (random coefficients): %d
", demo_problem$K2)) #> K2 (random coefficients): 4 cat(sprintf("D (demographics): %d
", demo_problem$D)) #> D (demographics): 4 cat(sprintf("Agents per market (from data): %d
", demo_problem$I)) #> Agents per market (from data): 1880 cat(sprintf("Sigma matrix: %d x %d
", demo_problem$K2, demo_problem$K2)) #> Sigma matrix: 4 x 4 cat(sprintf("Pi matrix: %d x %d
", demo_problem$K2, demo_problem$D)) #> Pi matrix: 4 x 4 # Sigma (4x4 diagonal): RC standard deviations # Starting values near pyblp's converged estimates sigma0 <- diag(c(0.558, 3.312, -0.006, 0.093))  # Pi (4x4): interactions between X2 characteristics and demographics # Rows = X2 vars (intercept, prices, sugar, mushy) # Cols = demographics (income, income_squared, age, child) pi0 <- matrix(c(   2.292, 0, 1.284, 0,        # intercept interactions   588.3, -30.19, 0, 11.05,   # price interactions   -0.384, 0, 0.0524, 0,      # sugar interactions   0.748, 0, -1.354, 0        # mushy interactions ), nrow = 4, ncol = 4, byrow = TRUE)  # Solve demo_results <- demo_problem$solve(   sigma = sigma0,   pi = pi0,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 1000, factr = 1e7)) )  print(demo_results) # Price coefficient ~ -63, matching pyblp's Nevo tutorial

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: Uses initial weighting matrix W=(Z′Z/N)−1W = (Z'Z/N)^{-1} (2SLS weight) throughout. produces consistent estimates asymptotically efficient.

title: Random Coefficients Logit
text: Step 1: Estimate 2SLS weight W1=(Z′Z/N)−1W_1 = (Z'Z/N)^{-1} Update: Compute optimal weight W*=Ŝ−1W^* = \hat{S}^{-1} Step 1 residuals Step 2: Re-estimate W*W^* Two-step GMM efficient (achieves smallest asymptotic variance among GMM estimators) can exhibit finite-sample bias, especially many moment conditions.
code: # One-step (faster, consistent) results_1s <- problem$solve(sigma = sigma0, method = "1s")  # Two-step (efficient, slower) results_2s <- problem$solve(sigma = sigma0, method = "2s")

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: random coefficients (RC) logit allows consumer heterogeneity preferences. consumer ii individual-specific taste coefficients: uijt=δjt+∑kxjkσkνik+εijtu_{ijt} = \delta_{jt} + \sum_k x_{jk} \sigma_k \nu_{ik} + \varepsilon_{ijt} νik\nu_{ik} standard normal draws σk\sigma_k captures dispersion tastes. Key parameters: integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3) uses Gauss-Hermite product rule 3 nodes per dimension (34=813^4 = 81 integration points per market) sigma K2 x K2 Cholesky root. Zeros diagonal fix parameter; non-zero values starting values optimization method = "2s" runs two-step efficient GMM (default); "1s" runs one-step
code: # X1: linear demand characteristics f1_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) # X2: random coefficient characteristics (same as X1 here) f2_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  rc_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1_rc, f2_rc),   product_data = products,   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3)  # Gauss-Hermite quadrature )  # Sigma: diagonal matrix with starting values (0 = fixed, non-zero = free) initial_sigma <- diag(c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5))  rc_results <- rc_problem$solve(   sigma = initial_sigma,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 200, factr = 1e7)) )  print(rc_results)

code: # Load agent (consumer) data with demographics agents <- load_nevo_agents() head(agents[, c("market_ids", "weights", "income", "age", "child")])  # Demographics formulation (no intercept) demo_form <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + income + income_squared + age + child)  # pyblp specification: X1 = '0 + prices' with product FE absorbed # X2 = '1 + prices + sugar + mushy' (with intercept) demo_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(     blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ product_ids),  # X1     blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)                # X2   ),   product_data = products,   agent_formulation = demo_form,   agent_data = agents )  # Sigma (4x4 diagonal) and Pi (4x4, K2 x D) # Starting values near pyblp's converged estimates sigma0 <- diag(c(0.558, 3.312, -0.006, 0.093)) pi0 <- matrix(c(   2.292, 0, 1.284, 0,   588.3, -30.19, 0, 11.05,   -0.384, 0, 0.0524, 0,   0.748, 0, -1.354, 0 ), nrow = 4, ncol = 4, byrow = TRUE)  demo_results <- demo_problem$solve(   sigma = sigma0, pi = pi0,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 1000, factr = 1e7)) )  print(demo_results) # Price coefficient ~ -63, matching pyblp's Nevo tutorial

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: Generate synthetic equilibrium data test estimator: Now estimate back true parameters:
code:
# Create a balanced panel: 50 markets, 20 products/market, 4 firms id_data <- build_id_data(T = 50, J = 20, F = 4) set.seed(42) id_data$x <- runif(nrow(id_data), 0, 1)  # True parameters: beta = (intercept=0.5, price=-2, x=0.8) sim <- blp_simulation(   product_formulations = list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x)),   product_data = id_data,   beta = c(0.5, -2, 0.8),   xi_variance = 0.3,   seed = 42 )  # Solve for equilibrium prices and shares sim_results <- sim$replace_endogenous(   iteration = blp_iteration("simple", list(atol = 1e-12, max_evaluations = 5000)) )  cat(sprintf("Price range: [%.3f, %.3f]
", min(sim_results$prices), max(sim_results$prices))) #> Price range: [1.550, 1.715] cat(sprintf("Share range: [%.5f, %.5f]
", min(sim_results$shares), max(sim_results$shares))) #> Share range: [0.00351, 0.15537] sim_problem <- sim_results$to_problem() sim_est <- sim_problem$solve(method = "1s")  cat("True vs Estimated:
") #> True vs Estimated: cat(sprintf("  intercept: true=0.50, est=%.4f
", sim_est$beta[1])) #>   intercept: true=0.50, est=-1.1803 cat(sprintf("  price:     true=-2.00, est=%.4f
", sim_est$beta[2])) #>   price:     true=-2.00, est=-0.9726 cat(sprintf("  x:         true=0.80, est=%.4f
", sim_est$beta[3])) #>   x:         true=0.80, est=0.8334

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: to_problem() method automatically constructs BLP instruments product data contain pre-computed instruments. can also construct instruments manually.

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: Traditional BLP instruments use sums characteristics -firm rival products: characteristic, BLP instruments produce two columns: : sum characteristic products firm rival: sum characteristic products rival firms valid instruments correlated prices (markup equation) uncorrelated unobserved quality ξ\xi (since characteristics exogenous).
code: # Extract the exogenous characteristic X_exog <- as.matrix(sim_data$product_data$x)  # Build BLP instruments blp_iv <- build_blp_instruments(   X = X_exog,   market_ids = sim_data$product_data$market_ids,   firm_ids = sim_data$product_data$firm_ids )  cat(sprintf("BLP instruments: %d columns\n", ncol(blp_iv))) #> BLP instruments: 2 columns cat("Column names:", paste(colnames(blp_iv), collapse = ", "), "\n") #> Column names: blp_own_x1, blp_rival_x1

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: Gandhi Houde (2020) propose instruments based product proximity characteristic space: local method counts number rival products within one standard deviation characteristic, quadratic method sums squared distances. Differentiation instruments can improve identification random coefficients capture degree product isolation, directly affects markups substitution patterns.
code: # Local differentiation instruments diff_iv_local <- build_differentiation_instruments(   X = X_exog,   market_ids = sim_data$product_data$market_ids,   firm_ids = sim_data$product_data$firm_ids,   method = "local" )  # Quadratic differentiation instruments diff_iv_quad <- build_differentiation_instruments(   X = X_exog,   market_ids = sim_data$product_data$market_ids,   firm_ids = sim_data$product_data$firm_ids,   method = "quadratic" )  cat(sprintf("Local diff. instruments: %d columns\n", ncol(diff_iv_local))) #> Local diff. instruments: 2 columns cat(sprintf("Quadratic diff. instruments: %d columns\n", ncol(diff_iv_quad))) #> Quadratic diff. instruments: 2 columns

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: RC logit simulation, specify sigma matrix (optionally pi integration):
code:
# RC logit DGP with 2 random coefficients id_data2 <- build_id_data(T = 30, J = 15, F = 3) set.seed(123) id_data2$x <- runif(nrow(id_data2), 0, 1)  rc_sim <- blp_simulation(   product_formulations = list(     blp_formulation(~ prices + x),  # X1: linear     blp_formulation(~ 0 + x)        # X2: nonlinear (RC on x only)   ),   product_data = id_data2,   beta = c(1.0, -3.0, 1.5),   # true linear parameters   sigma = matrix(0.8),         # true sigma for x (1x1)   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 5),   xi_variance = 0.5,   seed = 123 )  # Solve for equilibrium rc_sim_data <- rc_sim$replace_endogenous(   iteration = blp_iteration("simple", list(atol = 1e-12, max_evaluations = 5000)) )  # Estimate rc_sim_problem <- rc_sim_data$to_problem() rc_sim_results <- rc_sim_problem$solve(   sigma = matrix(0.5),  # starting value (not the true value)   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 200)) )  # Compare cat("True vs. Estimated:
") cat(sprintf("  beta:  true = (1.0, -3.0, 1.5), est = (%s)
",             paste(round(rc_sim_results$beta, 3), collapse = ", "))) cat(sprintf("  sigma: true = 0.8, est = %.3f
", rc_sim_results$sigma[1, 1]))

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: Monte Carlo study repeats simulate-estimate cycle many times study finite-sample properties estimator: bias, variance, coverage confidence intervals, etc.

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: Number replications: 500–1000 typical asymptotic properties; 100 may suffice debugging DGP parameters: choose values produce realistic shares prices Market/product structure: vary TT (markets), JJ (products/market), FF (firms) study sample size affects performance Instrument strength: compare BLP vs. differentiation instruments Integration accuracy: vary number nodes study simulation error Seeds: use different seed replication make results reproducible

what: Template for a Monte Carlo Study
title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
code:
# Monte Carlo study: logit estimator performance set.seed(2024) n_reps <- 100 true_beta <- c(0.5, -2, 0.8)  # Storage for results estimates <- matrix(NA, n_reps, 3) colnames(estimates) <- c("intercept", "price", "x")  for (rep in seq_len(n_reps)) {   # 1. Generate data   id_data_mc <- build_id_data(T = 50, J = 20, F = 4)   id_data_mc$x <- runif(nrow(id_data_mc), 0, 1)    sim_mc <- blp_simulation(     product_formulations = list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x)),     product_data = id_data_mc,     beta = true_beta,     xi_variance = 0.3,     seed = rep  # different seed each replication   )    sim_data_mc <- sim_mc$replace_endogenous(     iteration = blp_iteration("simple",       list(atol = 1e-12, max_evaluations = 5000))   )    # 2. Estimate   sim_prob_mc <- sim_data_mc$to_problem()   sim_res_mc <- sim_prob_mc$solve(method = "1s")    # 3. Store   estimates[rep, ] <- sim_res_mc$beta }  # 4. Summarize cat("Monte Carlo Results (", n_reps, " replications)
") cat("========================================
") for (k in seq_len(3)) {   cat(sprintf("  %s: true = %.2f, mean = %.4f, bias = %.4f, RMSE = %.4f
",               colnames(estimates)[k],               true_beta[k],               mean(estimates[, k]),               mean(estimates[, k]) - true_beta[k],               sqrt(mean((estimates[, k] - true_beta[k])^2)))) }

title: Simulation and Monte Carlo
text: simulation framework also supports supply-side estimation cost function. requires third formulation (X3) supply instruments:
code: # DGP with supply side id_data_supply <- build_id_data(T = 30, J = 15, F = 3) set.seed(99) id_data_supply$x <- runif(nrow(id_data_supply), 0, 1) id_data_supply$w <- runif(nrow(id_data_supply), 0.5, 1.5)  # cost shifter  supply_sim <- blp_simulation(   product_formulations = list(     blp_formulation(~ prices + x),   # X1: demand     blp_formulation(~ 0 + x),        # X2: RC on x     blp_formulation(~ x + w)         # X3: cost function   ),   product_data = id_data_supply,   beta = c(1.0, -3.0, 1.5),   sigma = matrix(0.5),   gamma = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.8),   # true cost parameters   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 5),   xi_variance = 0.3,   omega_variance = 0.2,   correlation = 0.7,  # correlation between xi and omega   seed = 99 )  supply_data <- supply_sim$replace_endogenous()  # The resulting problem will include supply-side estimation supply_problem <- supply_data$to_problem()
